# **Sundae Auto-Tuning Service Proposal**

Status: *In progress*  Author:  [Yi Chen](mailto:chen.y@salesforce.com)  Created: Sep 17, 2025  Last Updated: Sep 17, 2025

# Executive Summary

Today, most Spark job tuning happens at the individual job level. Early evidence from SUNDAE[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.8au7w7vn50fn) shows that resource consumption for selected jobs can be reduced significantly—yielding either cost savings in dollar terms or improved data landing time, but not both simultaneously[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.ej6o9thevvcq). In a multi-tenant environment, however, those freed resources are quickly re-allocated to other jobs, making the impact difficult for end-users to measure or justify. As a result, job owners have little incentive to tune, and the DI team cannot capture cost savings or SLA improvements at cluster scale[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.ej6o9thevvcq).

Recent progress on the SUNDAE project demonstrates that automated tuning is feasible and can reduce manual effort. However, the current approach still depends on user code changes and PR reviews, which limits adoption and dilutes the potential impact.

# Proposal

We propose building a **Spark Auto-Tuner Service (SATS)** , operated by DI, as part of the Spark-as-a-Service roadmap ([Spark as a Service 2028 Vision and Strategy](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1qLdoK6l-dItR2v3nj0VKVa2SQVj7widOy8T0uH1NXVo/edit). 

Key principles:

* **Zero code changes required** by job owners or framework teams (e.g. MF). Existing configs (e.g. spark.executor.memory) are treated as a hint, and the service tunes them safely.  
* **Measured at cluster scale**: impact will be validated through phased rollouts and versioned tuning configs, enabling DI to coordinate cost savings or SLA gains across clusters.  
* **Safety by design**: circuit-breakers will revert the config (without code changes) if tuning cause regression (task failures). 

# How it works

A very rough idea, not subject to critical review.

As part of the Spark-as-a-Service roadmap, we plan to decouple Airflow from the Spark infrastructure (EMR) by introducing a **Spark Submission Service (SSS)**. This service will handle job submissions and support long-term flexibility—for example, routing a job’s *first\_try* to future backends such as EMR 8 or Kubernetes.

The **Spark Auto-Tuner Service (SATS)** works alongside SSS:

* **Submission overrides:** At submission time, SSS can override user-specified Spark configs. SATS runs independently, managing its own planning, experimentation, and finalization phases.  
* **Persistence:** Once SATS finalizes new configs, it persists them in storage. SSS then applies these configs automatically at submission time and records state in its own store.  
* **Safe fallback:** If a job fails with SATS-recommended configs, the next submission automatically reverts to the user’s original hard-coded settings.

To safely measure impact and reduce onboarding risk, SATS introduces **phased, versioned tuning**:

* Each iteration of the tuning algorithm triggers a 30-day phased rollout, similar to online experimentation.  
* Daily, SATS tunes a subset of tasks, verifies results, then persists (with versioning) the configs before reverting to baseline.  
* After the 30-day cycle, SSS can enable the validated config version cluster-wide, allowing either coordinated cluster scale-down (cost savings) or retention of capacity to capture SLA improvements.

This design separates experimentation from production stability, enabling cluster-scale efficiency gains without requiring code changes from end-users. 

# Estimated Impact

Based on SUNDAE’s early results and one known benchmark, we expect **15%**[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.kqf9jqpfc8rp) cost savings or runtime improvements at cluster scale. This will vary by workload mix, but the key advantage is shifting benefits from “retail” tuning[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.ej6o9thevvcq) to aggregate cluster-level efficiency that can be easily measured either by dollar cost savings or by SLA improvements.

# F.A.Q

### *\[1\] What’s SUNDAE?*

See the recent announcement [here](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C8F0SQNSH/p1757350376112649). Screenshot is included below.![][image1]

### *\[2\] Why is it difficult to measure impact for retail-style, individual dag and task level tuning?*

It is not possible to measure the true impact of tuning a set of Spark jobs in a multi-tenant cluster. With so many competing factors at play, cost and SLA cannot be attributed cleanly to a single job’s (or a subset of jobs’) configuration. Most jobs run in shared queues, so any resource savings from one tuned job are quickly consumed by other untuned jobs. As a result, a 50% reduction in resource usage for a handful of jobs does not translate into 50% lower cluster costs or 2× faster SLAs. The only reliable way to assess impact is at the **cluster scale**, where aggregate effects can be measured.

### *\[3\] Why do we estimate a 15% cost savings?*

It is very difficult to estimate how much we can save from automatic tuning. However, Uber has implemented their own auto-tuning solution with a published result of 15% cost saving. If we assume that the Spark job quality at Slack is on par with Uber’s job quality in terms of tuning, we can estimate that we can achieve the same cost saving result. There are many variability at play. The goal of this estimate is to start with a baseline for reasoning and estimation. It is possible that the SUNDAE project itself can find a more accurate estimate before execution.
